title: Trump Extends Deadline for Iran to Reopen Strait of Hormuz to April 6
description: President Trump pushed back the deadline for Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz from Friday to April 6, warning of power grid strikes if Iran fails to comply.
imageAlt: Tensions continue in the Middle East as Trump extends deadline for Iran
lede: President Trump has again extended the deadline for Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, moving it from Friday to April 6. The announcement sent stocks falling sharply while oil prices rose on continued uncertainty over the critical transit route for global oil tankers.

sections.heading: What Happened
paragraphs: Trump moved the deadline for Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz — the world's most critical chokepoint for oil transport — from Friday to April 6, giving Tehran an additional 10 days to comply.
The extension comes amid ongoing U.S. and Israeli military operations in Iran. Trump had previously threatened strikes on Iran's power grid if the strait remained closed.

sections.heading: Market Impact
paragraphs: Stocks fell sharply on the announcement as investors weighed the extended uncertainty. Oil prices climbed as the threat of prolonged disruption to roughly 20% of the world's oil supply kept markets on edge.
The Strait of Hormuz handles approximately 21 million barrels of oil per day, making any disruption a major concern for global energy markets and consumer prices.

sections.heading: The Bigger Picture
paragraphs: The Iran conflict has already triggered fuel crises across Africa, with countries like South Sudan rationing electricity and Mauritius declaring an energy emergency after scheduled oil shipments failed to arrive.

U.S.-Iran talks have occurred in parallel with military operations, but no breakthrough has been reached. The extended deadline suggests Washington is leaving room for diplomatic resolution while maintaining military pressure.

sections.heading: What's Next
paragraphs: All eyes are now on April 6. If Iran fails to reopen the strait, Trump has signaled the U.S. would target Iran's power grid — an escalation that could deepen the humanitarian crisis and further destabilize global energy markets.
Diplomatic channels remain open, but the window for a peaceful resolution is narrowing with each deadline extension.

faqs.q: Why is the Strait of Hormuz important?
faqs.a: The Strait of Hormuz is a narrow waterway between Iran and Oman through which about 21 million barrels of oil pass daily — roughly 20% of global supply. Its closure disrupts energy markets worldwide.

faqs.q: What is Trump's deadline for Iran?
faqs.a: Trump extended the deadline to April 6, 2026 for Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. Failure to comply could result in U.S. strikes on Iran's power grid.

faqs.q: How are oil prices affected by the Iran conflict?
faqs.a: Oil prices have risen significantly due to supply disruption fears. The extended uncertainty over the Strait of Hormuz keeps markets volatile and pushes energy costs higher globally.

title: Zelensky Visits Saudi Arabia Offering Drone Expertise for Air Defense Missiles
description: Ukraine's president arrived in Saudi Arabia touting drone technology expertise in exchange for air defense missiles to fight Russia's invasion.
imageAlt: Ukrainian President Zelensky promotes Ukraine's drone technology expertise
lede: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky arrived in Saudi Arabia on Thursday, pitching Ukraine's battle-tested drone technology to Gulf states in exchange for air defense missiles desperately needed to counter Russia's ongoing invasion.

sections.heading: The Pitch
paragraphs: Zelensky offered Ukraine's expertise in drone technology as a way of improving security across the Gulf region, telling the Joint Expeditionary Force summit in Helsinki that energy security and the cost of living in Europe depend on stable oil and gas markets.

"The key is not only producing new weapons — especially drones — not just technology, but also real experience in using it, and integrating it with radars, aviation, and other air defence systems. We have this experience," Zelensky said.

sections.heading: What Ukraine Wants
paragraphs: In exchange for drone expertise, Zelensky is seeking air defense missiles from Middle Eastern states. "They have certain air defence missiles of which we don't have enough. That's what we'd like to reach a deal on," he told Le Monde.
Several Ukrainian drone companies told the BBC they have been approached by Gulf states — including Saudi Arabia and Kuwait — but are waiting for government approval to export.

sections.heading: Why It Matters
paragraphs: Ukraine has become the world's foremost testing ground for drone warfare, developing both offensive and defensive drone systems through years of combat against Russia. That hard-won expertise is now a valuable bargaining chip.
For Gulf states watching the Iran conflict unfold on their doorstep, Ukrainian anti-drone electronic warfare systems could be a critical defensive asset.

faqs.q: Why is Zelensky visiting Saudi Arabia?
faqs.a: Zelensky is offering Ukraine's drone technology expertise to Gulf states in exchange for air defense missiles needed to defend against Russia's invasion.

faqs.q: What drone technology does Ukraine have?
faqs.a: Ukraine has developed extensive drone warfare capabilities including offensive drones, anti-drone electronic warfare systems, and radar integration — all battle-tested in combat against Russia.

title: Canada Hits NATO's 2% Spending Target for First Time in Decades Under Carney
description: Canada finally reached NATO's military spending benchmark of 2% of GDP under PM Mark Carney, ending decades as a defense laggard.
imageAlt: Canadian military operations in the Arctic
lede: Canada has met NATO's 2% military spending target for the first time in decades, according to NATO's 2025 annual report released Thursday. Under Prime Minister Mark Carney, Canada embarked on its greatest military expansion since the Korean War.

sections.heading: The Milestone
paragraphs: NATO confirmed that Canada spent 2% of its gross domestic product on its military last year, meeting the threshold the alliance set in 2014. When that target was established, Canada's spending was roughly half the benchmark after accounting for inflation.
Canada also met a second NATO target: dedicating over 20% of military spending toward new equipment purchases.

sections.heading: Carney's Push
paragraphs: When Mark Carney became prime minister, he launched one of the greatest expansions of Canadian military spending since the Korean War. The push came amid mounting pressure from President Trump and shifting global security dynamics.

"We ran for office recognizing the world had changed, recognizing the importance of securing Canada, defending Canada, fulfilling our commitments," Carney said at a Royal Canadian Navy base in Halifax.

sections.heading: What's Next
paragraphs: NATO has since raised the military spending target to 5%, which Canada has pledged to meet by 2035. That would require a massive additional increase in defense budgets.

The spending milestone removes a longstanding point of friction between Canada and the United States, where successive presidents — including Trump — have criticized Canadian defense spending as inadequate.

faqs.q: Has Canada met the NATO spending target?
faqs.a: Yes. NATO's 2025 annual report confirmed Canada spent 2% of GDP on defense, meeting the target for the first time in decades under PM Mark Carney.

faqs.q: What is NATO's new spending target?
faqs.a: NATO raised the target to 5% of GDP. Canada has pledged to meet this by 2035.

title: IOC Limits Women's Olympic Events to Biological Females Starting 2028
description: The International Olympic Committee announced women's events will be restricted to biological females from the 2028 Los Angeles Olympics using SRY gene screening.
imageAlt: IOC President Kirsty Coventry announces new eligibility policy
lede: The International Olympic Committee will limit women's events to biological females starting with the 2028 Los Angeles Olympics, using a one-time SRY gene screening test to determine eligibility. The policy effectively bars transgender women and athletes with differences in sexual development who have gone through male puberty.

sections.heading: The New Policy
paragraphs: Eligibility for the female category will be determined by screening for the SRY gene — the sex-determining region Y gene on the Y chromosome that causes male characteristics to develop. Testing will use saliva, cheek swab, or blood samples.

Athletes who test negative permanently satisfy the eligibility criteria — the IOC calls it a "once-in-a-lifetime test." Those who test positive remain eligible for male categories, mixed events, and open categories.

sections.heading: Why Now
paragraphs: IOC President Kirsty Coventry said the policy was "led by medical experts" and cited competitive fairness. "At the Olympic Games even the smallest margins can be the difference between victory and defeat. It would not be fair for biological males to compete in the female category. In some sports it would simply not be safe."

Until this announcement, the IOC left sex eligibility to individual sport governing bodies rather than applying a universal rule. Sports like athletics, swimming, cycling, and rowing had already implemented their own bans.

sections.heading: The Debate
paragraphs: The decision formalizes what many individual sports had already done independently but creates a uniform standard across all Olympic events. It ends years of controversy that intensified after New Zealand's Laurel Hubbard became the first openly transgender woman to compete at an Olympics.
The policy takes effect for the Los Angeles 2028 Games and applies to all sports on the Olympic program.

faqs.q: Can transgender athletes compete in the 2028 Olympics?
faqs.a: Transgender women who have gone through male puberty cannot compete in women's events under the new IOC policy. They remain eligible for male, mixed, and open categories.

faqs.q: How will the IOC test athletes?
faqs.a: The IOC will use SRY gene screening via saliva, cheek swab, or blood sample. It's designed as a one-time test — athletes who pass never need to be tested again.

title: African Countries Ration Power and Dilute Fuel as Iran War Triggers Energy Crisis
description: South Sudan, Mauritius, Zimbabwe and other African nations impose emergency measures as the Iran conflict disrupts global fuel supplies.
imageAlt: Zimbabwe has increased ethanol in petrol to stretch fuel supplies
lede: The U.S.-Iran conflict is triggering an energy crisis across Africa, with South Sudan rationing electricity, Mauritius declaring an energy emergency, and Zimbabwe diluting petrol with ethanol to stretch dwindling fuel supplies.

sections.heading: Power Rationing Begins
paragraphs: South Sudan has begun rotating power cuts in Juba after its main electricity distributor cited the Iran conflict. The country generates 96% of its electricity from oil and imports most of its refined fuel despite having large oil reserves.

Mauritius declared an energy emergency after an expected oil shipment failed to arrive, leaving the island nation with only 21 days of reserves. Alternative supplies from Singapore are due April 1 — but at a higher cost.

sections.heading: Stretching Every Drop
paragraphs: Zimbabwe has increased the amount of ethanol blended into petrol to make supplies last longer. Across the continent, countries are scrambling to find alternative suppliers as traditional supply chains from the Middle East are disrupted.

In Juba, power often cuts out at 4 PM and doesn't return until 4 AM. "This paralyses most businesses," said electrical engineer Ereneo Mogga, adding that those who can afford it are switching to solar power.

sections.heading: Why Africa Is Hit Hardest
paragraphs: Many African nations depend heavily on oil imports for electricity generation and lack the strategic reserves that wealthier countries maintain. The Strait of Hormuz disruption has caused shipment delays and price spikes that disproportionately affect developing economies.
The crisis highlights Africa's vulnerability to geopolitical shocks in global energy markets and the urgent need for energy diversification.

faqs.q: Why is Africa facing a fuel crisis?
faqs.a: The U.S.-Iran conflict has disrupted oil shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, causing supply delays and price increases that disproportionately affect African countries dependent on oil imports.

faqs.q: Which African countries are rationing electricity?
faqs.a: South Sudan has begun rotating power cuts in Juba, and Mauritius declared an energy emergency with only 21 days of oil reserves remaining.

title: Air Canada CEO Apologizes for English-Only Condolence Video After LaGuardia Crash
description: Air Canada CEO Michael Rousseau faces resignation calls after failing to deliver condolences in French following a crash that killed French Canadian pilot.
imageAlt: Air Canada CEO Michael Rousseau faces criticism over language controversy
lede: Air Canada CEO Michael Rousseau apologized Thursday after facing calls to resign for delivering a condolence message only in English following a LaGuardia Airport crash that killed two pilots — one of whom was French Canadian.

sections.heading: The Controversy
paragraphs: After an Air Canada plane collided with a fire truck at LaGuardia Airport on Sunday night, killing pilots Antoine Forest and Mackenzie Gunther, Rousseau released a video statement expressing condolences — entirely in English with French subtitles.
The English-only delivery sparked outrage in Quebec, particularly because one of the killed pilots, Antoine Forest, was French Canadian. The flight had come from Montreal.

sections.heading: Political Fallout
paragraphs: Prime Minister Mark Carney called the English-only message "a lack of compassion." Quebec Premier François Legault said Rousseau should resign if he cannot speak French.

Canada's parliamentary Committee on Official Languages summoned Rousseau to explain. In a written statement released in both English and French, Rousseau admitted his French remains weak "despite many lessons over several years."

sections.heading: The Bigger Picture
paragraphs: Language politics remain a sensitive issue in Canada, where both English and French are official languages. For the CEO of a national airline — named Air Canada — to be unable to address the country in both languages touches a deep cultural nerve.
Rousseau's statement did not address the resignation calls. Dozens of passengers were injured in the crash, with four still in hospital.

faqs.a: An Air Canada plane operated by Jazz Aviation collided with a fire truck after landing on Sunday night, killing both pilots — Antoine Forest and Mackenzie Gunther. Dozens of passengers were injured.

faqs.q: Why is the Air Canada CEO facing criticism?
faqs.a: CEO Michael Rousseau delivered a condolence video only in English, sparking outrage because one of the killed pilots was French Canadian and the flight originated from Montreal. Canada's PM called it 'a lack of compassion.'
